content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

What the web’s most-used CMS looks like when you actually count it.About webatla. webatla is a domain-intelligence platform. We crawl and fingerprint domains across the web and record which technology each site runs — the same idea as BuiltWith or Wappalyzer, with broader coverage. Every figure here is a real count from the webatla index, last refreshed 6 July 2026. The full write-up, with all charts, lives on the blog linked above.
Most sentences about WordPress start with a vibe: it’s everywhere, it powers half the internet, everyone uses it. This one starts with a count. There are 23,589,840 websites created using WordPress live in the webatla index — 7.7% of every domain we track. That is the number this piece is about: where those sites live, what their domains say about them, and why the data needs to be read with a little care.
The count, and what it beats
23,589,840 is not an estimate rounded up from a survey. It is the number of distinct live domains where webatla currently detects WordPress. To give it scale: it is 3.3× the next website platform, Squarespace (7,240,492 sites), and it is larger than Wix, Shopify, Joomla and Squarespace combined. When people say the web runs on WordPress, this is the concrete version of that claim — one CMS holding a 7.7% slice of everything indexed, with every rival in single digits or lower.
What the domain endings tell you
A useful way to read a population of 23 million sites is to look at where their domains resolve. The top-level domain (TLD) split is a rough map of who builds with WordPress and where.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Top TLDs among WordPress sites. Source: webatla index, July 2026.Nearly half of all sites built with WordPress — 11,116,459 domains, 47.1% — sit on .com. After that the list is unmistakably international: .de (5.5%), .org (4.1%), .uk (2.9%) and .br (2.7%). That .de showing in second place is not noise — it mirrors the country data, where Germany is the second-largest market for websites made using WordPress after the United States, ahead of France, Japan and the Netherlands. The .org share is the tell for the platform's civic and nonprofit roots: WordPress began as a publishing tool, and a meaningful chunk of the web's associations, projects and communities still run on it.
The short read: sites powered by WordPress are overwhelmingly commercial (.com), heavily European in the long tail, and still carry a visible .org publishing heritage. That is a different fingerprint from a hosted store platform like Shopify, whose footprint skews toward retail.
Half of them run one release
Here is a detail that surprises people. Among all live installs, the WordPress version is extraordinarily concentrated: per webatla’s fingerprinting, 49.2% report version 6.9.4 — a single release covering nearly half of every website created using WordPress. Another 18.2% sit on the 7.0 branch, with the rest thinly spread across 6.8.5, 6.7.5 and 6.6.5. For anyone reasoning about security or plugin compatibility, that concentration is a gift: understand one dominant release and you have effectively characterized half the WordPress web.
The part where the data needs honesty
Now the caveat, because a count is only as good as your willingness to read it critically. The webatla index reports a -66.17% year-over-year change in detected live WordPress installs. Left unexamined, that reads like a collapse. It isn’t — and the way to know is to look sideways at everything else in the same snapshot.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Year-over-year change is index-wide, not WordPress-specific. Source: webatla index, July 2026.WordPress dropped 66.2% year over year — but so did WooCommerce (-61.7%), Yoast SEO (-75.5%), Astra (-45.0%), LiteSpeed Cache (-37.8%) and Drupal (-87.8%). Unrelated technologies do not all shed half their installs in the same window for the same real-world reason. When an entire class of detections moves together, the cause is the index recounting — a change in how live installations are measured and fingerprinted between crawls — not tens of millions of sites switching off. The number that survives that scrutiny, and the one worth quoting, is the share: 7.7% of the indexed web, and a 3.3× lead over the next platform.
What kinds of sites these are
The plugin layer answers “what are all these sites, really.” The most common add-ons are Elementor (7,887,817 sites) and Yoast SEO (7,333,886) — a page builder and an SEO plugin, which is the signature of content and marketing sites built to rank. Underneath sits a large commerce segment: WooCommerce turns 3,377,108 of these installs into live stores. So the honest one-line description of the WordPress web is: mostly content sites built to be found, with a large e-commerce layer, running on hosts that increasingly ship caching like LiteSpeed Cache.
Where these sites live
Domain endings hint at geography; the country data confirms it. Here is where websites made by WordPress actually resolve:
CountryWordPress sitesShareUnited States9,495,69640.8%Germany3,339,66614.3%France1,194,8825.1%Japan1,053,9314.5%Netherlands729,9703.1%
The United States alone holds four in ten sites created with WordPress, and the top five countries together account for roughly two-thirds of the total. It is a heavily Western, commercially weighted map — the same shape the .com and .de TLD split predicted a few paragraphs up. The data agrees with itself, which is a small but reassuring sign that the count is measuring something real.
How the count is made
It is worth being explicit about method, because a headline number invites the question “counted how?” webatla does not poll a panel or extrapolate from a sample. We crawl domains at web scale and fingerprint the technologies each site serves — the WordPress core, the theme, and the plugin stack — then tally the distinct live domains carrying that fingerprint. So 23,589,840 is a direct count of detections, not a modeled estimate. The plugin and version numbers come from the same crawl, which is why a WooCommerce or Elementor figure is always a clean subset of the WordPress total rather than a separate guess.
The commerce footnote most people miss
WordPress gets filed under “blogging,” but its store count says otherwise. WooCommerce is live on 3,377,108 sites — each a real storefront — which is more than standalone Shopify at 2,746,823. Put plainly: more of the indexed web’s online stores are built on WordPress than on the platform whose entire brand is e-commerce. That single comparison rewires how you should picture the WordPress web — it is not just publishing, it is one of the largest commerce surfaces online.
What the number is actually good for
A count like this only earns its place if you do something with it. If you are sizing a market, 7.7% tells you WordPress is the base case for any random site you will meet. If you are choosing a stack, the version concentration tells you compatibility is more predictable than the sprawl of plugins first suggests. And if you are watching trends, the year-over-year lesson is to distrust any single delta until you have checked whether the whole index moved with it. The real value of counting 23,589,840 websites created using WordPress is not the figure itself — it is that every claim stacked on top of it can be checked against the same crawl, instead of taken on faith.
How to get the list of websites using WordPress
You can work with this data directly instead of quoting a chart. The WordPress technology page on webatla is a live, filterable list of every site we detect — filter by country, TLD and version, and cross-reference the ecosystem plugins above. The complete write-up, with the country breakdown and every chart, is on the source article: webatla.com/blog/websites-created-using-wordpress.
